subject: Company Law
module_title: Corporate Decision-Making: Resolutions, Meetings and Board Governance
lesson_title: Types of Resolutions and the Duomatic Principle

questions.question: Under CA 2014, an ordinary resolution requires what majority threshold, and what minimum notice period applies where the articles are silent?

options.A: More than 50% of votes cast; 21 days notice
options.B: More than 50% of votes cast; 7 days notice for a private company
options.C: At least 50% of votes cast (simple majority); 14 days notice

questions.explanation: An ordinary resolution requires a simple majority — more than 50% of votes cast by entitled members. Under CA 2014, for a private limited company, the minimum statutory notice for a general meeting is generally 7 days (subject to the articles). Special notice (28 days) applies only to specific resolutions such as s.146 removal of directors.

questions.question: A special resolution under CA 2014 requires a 75% majority. Under s.198 CA 2014, within what period after passing a special resolution must a company file a copy with the Companies Registration Office (CRO)?
questions.explanation: Under s.198 CA 2014, a company must deliver a copy of every special resolution to the CRO within 15 days of it being passed. Failure to do so is a Category 3 offence for the company and any officer in default.

questions.question: The Duomatic principle (from Re Duomatic Ltd [1969] Ch) allows informal shareholder consent to substitute for a formal general meeting resolution. For the principle to operate in Irish law, which conditions must ALL be met?

options.A: The shareholders must have actual knowledge of the relevant matter and give unanimous informal consent; all shareholders entitled to attend and vote must participate
options.B: A majority of shareholders must give informal consent in writing, provided no shareholder objects within 21 days
options.C: The shareholders must give unanimous consent in a board meeting attended by all shareholders
options.D: The consent of shareholders holding at least 75% of the issued shares is sufficient to trigger the Duomatic principle

questions.explanation: The Duomatic principle requires: (1) unanimous consent of ALL shareholders entitled to attend and vote; (2) actual knowledge of the relevant matter; and (3) a genuine assent (not merely passive acquiescence). An absent shareholder whose consent is not obtained prevents operation of the principle. Confirmed in Ireland: Kerr v Conduit Enterprises Ltd [2010] IEHC.

questions.question: In Kerr v Conduit Enterprises Ltd [2010] IEHC, the Irish High Court applied the Duomatic principle. What was the key factual scenario, and what did the court confirm about the principle's operation in Irish law?

options.A: The court confirmed that a majority shareholder's informal consent is sufficient; minority shareholders cannot veto informal decision-making
options.B: The court confirmed that where all shareholders with voting rights are informed of and assent to a particular course of action — even without a formal meeting — that assent has the same legal effect as a formal resolution
options.C: The court held that the Duomatic principle applies only in liquidation proceedings and not to decisions taken by a going concern
options.D: The court restricted the Duomatic principle to decisions that would otherwise require only an ordinary resolution — special resolution matters require formal compliance

questions.explanation: In Kerr v Conduit Enterprises [2010] IEHC, the High Court confirmed that the Duomatic principle operates in Irish company law. Where all shareholders entitled to vote are fully informed and informally assent to a decision, that assent is legally equivalent to a formally passed resolution, provided unanimity is established.

questions.question: A company has four shareholders. Three shareholders informally assent to paying a director's bonus outside the authorised remuneration structure. The fourth shareholder was unaware of the decision. Can the Duomatic principle validate this decision?

options.A: Yes — 75% assent is sufficient to trigger Duomatic where the matter would ordinarily require only an ordinary resolution
options.B: No — the Duomatic principle requires the informed assent of ALL shareholders entitled to vote. The fourth shareholder's unawareness means there was no unanimous consent and the principle cannot operate
options.C: Yes — a shareholder who fails to attend board meetings is deemed to have waived their right to object to informal decisions
options.D: Yes — provided the majority can demonstrate the decision was in the company's best interests, the courts will validate it even without unanimous consent

questions.explanation: The Duomatic principle requires UNANIMITY — the informed assent of every shareholder entitled to vote. A dissenting or uninformed shareholder prevents its operation absolutely. The court in Buchanan v McVey [1954] IR confirmed this strict unanimity requirement.

questions.question: Under s.161 CA 2014, a private limited company may pass a written resolution. Which of the following resolutions CANNOT be passed as a written resolution under s.161?

options.A: A resolution to increase the company's authorised share capital
options.B: A special resolution to alter the articles of association
options.C: An ordinary resolution to remove a director under s.146 CA 2014
options.D: A resolution to approve a contract under s.238 CA 2014

questions.explanation: Under s.161 CA 2014, written resolutions may be used for most decisions. However, s.161(2) expressly excepts resolutions under s.146 (removal of a director) from the written resolution procedure — such removal must be effected at a general meeting with 28-day special notice. Similarly, s.397 (removal of auditors) cannot be done by written resolution.

questions.question: A private limited company passes a written resolution under s.161 CA 2014. What majority threshold is required, and how is it calculated?

options.A: The same majority as would be required at a general meeting — ordinary written resolutions require more than 50% of total voting rights; special written resolutions require 75% of total voting rights
options.B: Unanimous consent of all shareholders is required for all written resolutions regardless of type
options.C: Written resolutions require more than 50% of votes actually cast by those who respond within the circulation period
options.D: Written resolutions always require 75% of all issued voting shares regardless of the type of resolution they replace

questions.explanation: Under s.161(4)-(5) CA 2014, an ordinary written resolution requires the agreement of members holding more than 50% of total voting rights, and a special written resolution requires 75% of total voting rights. Crucially, non-responding shareholders count as votes against — the calculation is of total voting rights, not just those who respond.

questions.question: A shareholder of a private company holds 30% of shares and wishes to block a special resolution to alter the articles. The majority (70%) proposes to use a written resolution under s.161 CA 2014. Can the 30% shareholder prevent the special written resolution from passing?

options.A: No — a written resolution requires only a simple majority among those who respond; if the majority responds and the minority does not, the resolution passes
options.B: Yes — a special written resolution requires 75% of total voting rights. A 30% shareholder, by refusing to sign or not responding, means the resolution cannot achieve the 75% threshold needed
options.C: It depends — if the 70% holder responds within the time limit, the resolution passes regardless of non-response by others
options.D: No — the company can apply to court for an order deeming the resolution passed if the minority is deliberately obstructing the process

questions.explanation: Under s.161(5) CA 2014, a special written resolution requires 75% of total voting rights. A shareholder holding 30% who does not sign (or sign against) means the maximum possible 'yes' vote is 70% — below the 75% threshold. The 30% shareholder can therefore block any special written resolution by withholding their signature.

questions.question: A company passes a resolution by the Duomatic principle to pay the sole director a salary significantly above the level approved in the articles. The company subsequently goes into liquidation. The liquidator seeks to recover the excess salary. The director argues the Duomatic principle validates the arrangement. The company had only two shareholders — the director and their spouse. What is the critical factual question?

options.A: Whether the salary was commercially justifiable having regard to market rates for comparable directors
options.B: Whether the spouse, as a co-shareholder, was fully aware of and genuinely assented to the arrangement — and whether this assent was truly independent, or whether in practice the spouse simply deferred to the director
options.C: Whether the director disclosed their interest in the remuneration arrangement to the board in accordance with s.228(1)(g) CA 2014
options.D: Whether the salary was approved in board minutes signed by both shareholders

questions.explanation: For the Duomatic principle to operate, all shareholders must give genuine, informed, and independent assent. Where the second shareholder is the director's spouse, courts will scrutinise whether the spouse truly exercised independent judgment or merely deferred to the director. A 'rubber stamp' assent by a dominated shareholder does not satisfy the principle's requirements.

questions.question: Under the Duomatic principle as applied in Buchanan v McVey [1954] IR, is it possible for shareholders to ratify a breach of duty informally, even if a formal meeting would be required for such ratification?

options.A: No — ratification of director breaches can only occur at a properly convened extraordinary general meeting with formal notice
options.B: Yes — provided all shareholders with voting rights are fully informed of the breach and unanimously assent to ratification, the Duomatic principle allows informal ratification with the same effect as a formal resolution
options.C: Yes — but only where the breach was of a fiduciary duty; contractual breaches always require formal shareholder approval
options.D: No — the Duomatic principle cannot operate where a conflict of interest exists between the director and the company

questions.explanation: In Buchanan v McVey [1954] IR, the Irish Supreme Court affirmed that informal unanimous assent by all shareholders can ratify director conduct with the same effect as a formal general meeting resolution. The principle applies to ratification of breaches of duty, subject to the requirement of full information and genuine unanimous consent.

questions.question: Special notice under s.146(3) CA 2014 must be given before a resolution to remove a director can be placed on the agenda of a general meeting. Which of the following correctly describes the special notice procedure?

options.A: 28 days' notice must be given to the company by a member intending to move the resolution; the company must then give at least 21 days' notice to all members and to the director concerned
options.B: 14 days' notice must be given to the company; the company then calls an EGM with 7 days' notice
options.C: The member must give 21 days' notice to the company and the company must give 28 days' notice to the director personally
options.D: 28 days' notice must be given to the company; the company must give the director at least 28 days' notice and send the director's representations to all members

questions.explanation: Under s.146(3) CA 2014, special notice of 28 days must be given by the member to the company. On receiving special notice, the company must give the director 28 days' notice and, if the director wishes to make representations, circulate those representations to all members before the meeting. This ensures procedural fairness for the director facing removal.

questions.question: A company's articles are silent on the quorum for shareholder meetings. Under s.182 CA 2014, what is the default quorum for a general meeting of a private limited company?

options.A: Three members present in person or by proxy
options.B: Two members present in person or by proxy
options.C: A majority of all members entitled to vote
options.D: One member present in person where the company is a single-member company; two members for all other private companies

questions.explanation: Under s.182 CA 2014, the default quorum for a general meeting of a private limited company is two members present in person or by proxy. For a single-member company, quorum is one member. The articles may specify a higher quorum.

questions.question: Can the Duomatic principle validate a decision that would, if taken formally, constitute an illegal act or a fraud on the minority? Apply the authorities.

options.A: Yes — the Duomatic principle is absolute and validates any informal unanimous shareholder decision regardless of its subject matter
options.B: No — the Duomatic principle cannot validate acts that are illegal, beyond the company's capacity, or constitute a fraud on the minority or a breach that cannot be ratified even by unanimous shareholders
options.C: Yes — provided all shareholders are the individuals perpetrating the alleged fraud, they can consent to their own wrong using Duomatic
options.D: The Duomatic principle has no application where the company has creditors — their interests must be considered in all shareholder decisions

questions.explanation: The Duomatic principle operates within the same constraints as formal shareholder resolutions. It cannot validate illegal acts, ultra vires acts, or fraud on minority shareholders. It also cannot ratify conduct that would harm creditors in the zone of insolvency. Unanimous shareholder consent is not omnipotent: Cook v Deeks [1916] limits on ratification apply equally to informal Duomatic consent.

questions.question: A resolution to wind up a company voluntarily under s.580 CA 2014 (members' voluntary liquidation) requires a special resolution. Can this be passed by written resolution under s.161 CA 2014?

options.A: No — winding-up resolutions must always be passed at a physically convened general meeting
options.B: Yes — a special written resolution under s.161 can be used to resolve to wind up voluntarily, provided the 75% threshold of total voting rights is met
options.C: No — a voluntary winding-up resolution requires unanimous consent and cannot be achieved by the s.161 written resolution procedure
options.D: Only if the company has fewer than 10 shareholders — larger companies must use a formal general meeting

questions.explanation: A members' voluntary liquidation requires a special resolution under s.580 CA 2014. s.161 CA 2014 permits special resolutions to be passed by written resolution, subject to the 75% threshold. There is no specific exception for winding-up resolutions (unlike s.146 director removal and s.397 auditor removal). Therefore, a special written resolution is competent for this purpose.

questions.question: Under s.194 CA 2014, a resolution passed at a general meeting must be minuted by the company secretary. Within what period must the minutes be signed by the chairperson of the meeting or the next succeeding meeting, and what is the evidential effect of signed minutes?

options.A: Minutes must be signed within 21 days; signed minutes are conclusive evidence that the meeting was properly held and all resolutions passed
options.B: There is no fixed time limit, but once signed by the chairperson, minutes are prima facie evidence of the proceedings at the meeting — they are not conclusive
options.C: Minutes must be signed within 7 days and are conclusive evidence of the proceedings
options.D: Minutes must be signed within 28 days; unsigned minutes have no evidential value in legal proceedings

questions.explanation: Under s.194 CA 2014, once signed by the chairperson of the meeting at which the proceedings are recorded or the next succeeding meeting, the minutes are prima facie evidence of the proceedings. They are not conclusive — they may be rebutted by contrary evidence.

questions.question: A shareholders' agreement requires unanimity for any amendment to the agreement. The majority shareholders use the articles amendment procedure (special resolution, 75%) to alter the articles in a way that conflicts with the shareholders' agreement. What is the legal effect?

options.A: The special resolution prevails — statutory procedures override any contractual arrangement between shareholders
options.B: The articles amendment is effective as a matter of company law (having passed the required threshold), but the majority shareholders who voted for it may be in breach of the shareholders' agreement and liable in damages to the minority
options.C: The amendment is void — a shareholders' agreement requiring unanimity effectively prevents any alteration of the articles without unanimous consent
options.D: The courts will always strike down an articles amendment that conflicts with an earlier shareholders' agreement

questions.explanation: A special resolution to amend the articles is effective as a matter of company law regardless of any conflicting shareholders' agreement. However, the shareholders who voted for the amendment in breach of the unanimity requirement in the shareholders' agreement are liable in breach of contract. The shareholders' agreement creates personal contractual obligations but cannot be entrenched against statutory procedures: Russell v Northern Bank Development [1992] HL.

questions.question: Niltan Carson v Hawthorne [1988] IEHC (Ireland) is an authority on quorum defects at shareholder meetings. What was the key holding?

options.A: A quorum defect renders resolutions voidable at the company's option but not automatically void
options.B: A meeting that was quorate when it opened but became inquorate mid-meeting renders subsequent resolutions void
options.C: A meeting held without a proper quorum renders all resolutions passed at that meeting void — approval at an inquorate meeting has no legal effect under CA 2014
options.D: A quorum defect may be cured by the later Duomatic assent of absentee shareholders who would have made up the quorum

questions.explanation: In Niltan Carson v Hawthorne [1988] IEHC, Costello J held that resolutions passed at a meeting lacking the required quorum are void — not merely voidable. The case is particularly significant as it arose in the context of s.238 CA 2014 precursors, confirming that procedural defects in approval procedures have substantive legal consequences.
